📍How to Use
🌐中
⚡ Advanced edition · 2026 real-world scenarios

Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat
Advanced Practice Handbook (Pro)

16 advanced scenarios drawn from real 2026 work situations across strategic decisions, data insights, content creation, and process automation. Every scenario supports multi-round prompt iteration and showcases advanced prompting techniques.

16Advanced scenarios
4Capability tracks
36Iteration rounds
10Sample files

📘 How to Use

  1. Open Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat.
  2. Each scenario contains 1–3 rounds of prompt iteration; every round is tagged with the advanced technique used (role-play, structured templates, file grounding, cross-regulation mapping, etc.).
  3. Click 📋 Copy to copy that round's prompt and paste it into the Copilot Chat input.
  4. For scenarios that need a sample file, upload the corresponding file from the samples\ folder.
  5. Start a fresh "New chat" before each new scenario, but the multi-round prompts within the same scenario must be sent in the same conversation.
  6. All company names have been anonymized (e.g., "Tier-1 foundry A (anonymized)") so the deck is safe to demo on customer sites.
01
♟️

Competitive Intelligence Matrix (multi-round)

Strategic Decisions

A 3-round iteration showing how to converge a vague question into a decision-ready competitive comparison and re-pitch it for a different audience. Scenario: the three leading 2nm-class foundry camps.

💡
Advanced techniques: ① Role priming (assign expert persona) ② Dimension stacking (5 dimensions) ③ Source-tier tagging ④ Audience switch and rewrite
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Initial rough question
❌ First-draft demo 
Help me compare the pros and cons of the 2nm processes from the world's three largest foundry camps (A, B, C).
Round 1 sample output · 01_competitive_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Structured rewrite
✅ Role priming + dimension constraints + structured output 
You are the chief semiconductor analyst at a global investment bank, preparing an institutional client briefing. Compile a competitive analysis of the 2nm-class advanced process race among "Tier-1 Asian foundry A (anonymized)", "US-based IDM player B (anonymized)", and "Korean memory + foundry player C (anonymized)" as a 5-dimension comparison matrix (Markdown table):
1. Mass-production timeline (with disclosed customer tape-out progress)
2. PPA metrics (power / performance / density vs. 3nm baseline)
3. Confirmed customers and order scale
4. Yield and ramp risk (provide confidence level)
5. Geopolitical exposure

For each cell, tag the source tier (A=official filings/earnings call, B=credible media, C=analyst estimate). End with a three-sentence investment conclusion.
Round 2 sample output · 01_competitive_v2.png
R3Round 3 · Audience switch
✅ Audience switch + constraints 
Rewrite the analysis above for "a non-technical independent board director": drop all acronyms and jargon, use analogies, keep it under 250 words, and finish with a single sentence telling them "whether our company should place an order".
Round 3 sample output · 01_competitive_v3.png
↑ Back to top
02
🌏

Market Entry Assessment: PESTEL + TAM

Strategic Decisions

A 2-round market evaluation for "Major ODM B (anonymized) building a southbound AI-server line" — first frame the analysis, then stress-test it.

💡
Advanced techniques: ① Three-part structure ② Uncertainty tagging ③ Red-team self-rebuttal (raises decision quality)
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Framework analysis
✅ Few-shot structure + numerical requirements 
Act as a senior partner at a global strategy consulting firm. For Major ODM B (a leading Taiwan ODM, anonymized), evaluate the market-entry decision to "set up an AI-server assembly plant in a southern India tech park in 2027".

Deliver in three parts (each section must include concrete numbers; mark unverified items with [needs verification]):

【Part A · Market sizing】
- TAM: India 2027–2030 AI-server market USD size and CAGR
- SAM: serviceable hyperscale + enterprise segments
- SOM: at 8% market share, estimate the first 3 years of revenue / EBITDA

【Part B · PESTEL scoring】
Score each of the 6 factors from -2 to +2, with one sentence of rationale, then sum to a total score.

【Part C · Top 3 risks and mitigations】
Quantify each risk's probability and potential financial impact.
Round 1 sample output · 02_market_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Red-team stress test
✅ Red-team self-critique 
Now switch to the role of "a hedge-fund short-side analyst". Challenge the three weakest assumptions in the report you just produced. For each assumption, provide one piece of counter-evidence and re-estimate the downside risk.
Round 2 sample output · 02_market_v2.png
↑ Back to top
03
🚨

Cybersecurity Crisis Talking Points

Strategic Decisions

A listed e-commerce company has 8 hours to go public after a data breach. 3 rounds iterate three audience-tailored scripts and tune the tone.

📎
Sample file: samples/crisis_brief.docx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① File grounding ② Role-play as adversary for stress test ③ Emotion / tone fine-tuning
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Draft from the incident brief
✅ File grounding + role 
You are the crisis-communications director at a global PR consultancy. Based on the attached "IR-2026-0411 Cybersecurity Incident Brief", produce three layers of talking points (each under 200 words):

A. To the media (press conference statement)
B. To affected members (SMS + email notification)
C. To all employees (internal letter)

Each piece must include: confirmed facts, actions already taken, commitments, and next steps. Tone: "sincere but avoid committing to compensation amounts".
Round 1 sample output · 03_crisis_v1.png
R2Round 2 · 8 sharp Q&A stress test
✅ Simulated Q&A + adversarial test 
Now act as a reporter from a major financial media outlet. List the 8 sharpest questions you would ask the CEO at the press conference (covering breach details, accountability, compensation, board responsibility, whether police were notified, links to overseas subsidiaries, why credit cards were not exposed, and why disclosure took 8 hours). For each question, provide the recommended "three-sentence standard answer" for the CEO plus the "absolute red lines" they must not say.
Round 2 sample output · 03_crisis_v2.png
R3Round 3 · Calibrate the executive's tone
✅ Tone tuning + emotional cues 
After reading the Q&A, the CEO says, "This feels too cold — I want people to feel I genuinely care about our customers." Rewrite the press-conference statement into a version that is "human, warm, but still professional", and add a personal opening from the CEO (no more than 80 words).
Round 3 sample output · 03_crisis_v3.png
↑ Back to top
04
📝

BLUF Memo for Senior Leadership

Strategic Decisions

Compress a Q1 financial pack into a BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front) memo for the CFO. 2 rounds demonstrate structured output.

📎
Sample file: samples/q1_results.xlsx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① BLUF structural prompt ② Specify output fields (incl. risks / recommendations)
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · BLUF structure
✅ Structured template 
Use the BLUF (Bottom Line Up Front) format to analyze the attached Q1 results for the CFO, in under 500 words. Structure:

【Bottom Line】One sentence
【Three Key Numbers】Bullets
【Beat / Miss】2 bullets each
【Three Risk Signals】
【Recommended Next Steps】3 actionable items

Tone: direct, no hedging, let the numbers speak.
Round 1 sample output · 04_bluf_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Add a What-if scenario
✅ Scenario modeling 
Continuing from the memo above, assume that in Q2 the USD strengthens by 5% and the semiconductor cycle deteriorates by 10%. Add a "Q2 stress-test scenario" section that estimates the impact on revenue and gross margin and recommends 3 hedging actions.
Round 2 sample output · 04_bluf_v2.png
↑ Back to top
05
🔬

Semiconductor OSAT Capacity Analysis

Data Insights

Analyze Q4 revenue and utilization across three OSAT players and derive an investment view. 2 rounds move from descriptive numbers to a thesis.

📎
Sample file: samples/semicon_q4.xlsx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① Multi-sheet Excel grounding ② Move from description to investment thesis
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Three-way comparison
✅ Multi-sheet comparison 
The attached file contains 2025 Q4 monthly revenue and capacity utilization for three OSAT players (X, Y, Z). Please:
1. Each player's QoQ / YoY growth
2. Compare the three players' utilization trends (describe the trend in prose)
3. Which one is most worth watching, and why?
4. Present the summary in a single Markdown table
Round 1 sample output · 05_semi_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Investment thesis
✅ Role + framework output 
You are now the semiconductor analyst at a global investment bank. Based on the data above, write an "investor flash note" (under 300 words) that includes:
- Rating for each player (Buy / Hold / Sell)
- The biggest upside catalyst and downside risk for each
- 12-month view: which one is the top pick? Provide 3 reasons

Note: clearly state that this is a preliminary view based on limited data and is not investment advice.
Round 2 sample output · 05_semi_v2.png
↑ Back to top
06
🌱

IFRS S2 Sustainability Disclosure

Data Insights

Map Scope 1/2/3 emissions to IFRS S2 disclosure requirements and produce a draft section for the sustainability report. 3 rounds demonstrate compliance + localization + quantitative scenarios.

📎
Sample file: samples/emissions_data.xlsx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① Regulatory grounding ② Localization (FSC Taiwan, SBTi) ③ Quantitative scenarios (NGFS scenarios)
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · S2 mapping
✅ Regulatory grounding 
The attached file contains Major ODM B's 2024-2025 Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG emissions data. Per IFRS S2 paragraph 29 "climate-related metrics" requirements, produce a compliant disclosure section (in English, under 1,500 words) that includes:
- Absolute Scope 1/2/3 emissions and emission intensity
- Gap vs. the SBTi 1.5°C pathway
- Top 3 categories within Scope 3 by share
- Mix of tables and prose
Round 1 sample output · 06_s2_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Add FSC Taiwan local disclosures
✅ Stack local regulations 
The above is the international version. Now, per the 2026 edition of FSC Taiwan's "Regulations Governing the Preparation and Filing of Sustainability Reports by Listed Companies", add:
- Board-level climate governance structure
- Internal carbon pricing (recommend a USD/ton range with rationale)
- Climate-related scenario analysis (must include at least the NGFS Net Zero 2050 and Delayed Transition scenarios)
Use a mix of bullets and prose.
Round 2 sample output · 06_s2_v2.png
R3Round 3 · Quantitative scenarios
✅ Advanced quantitative modeling 
Using NGFS Net Zero 2050 and Delayed Transition scenarios, quantitatively estimate to 2030:
- Projected Scope 1+2 emissions (tons CO2e)
- Carbon fee / tax cost impact (assume Taiwan carbon fee starting at USD 30/ton)
- Range of impact on gross margin (percentage points)
Present a 4-column table (scenario / emissions / carbon cost / margin impact), and end with a 200-word conclusion.
Round 3 sample output · 06_s2_v3.png
↑ Back to top
07
💬

VOC Analysis: 200 App Reviews

Data Insights

From 200 app reviews, extract themes, sentiment scores, and product improvement ideas. 2 rounds move from classification to a PM action plan.

📎
Sample file: samples/app_reviews.csv
💡
Advanced techniques: ① Theme extraction from large CSV ② Jump to PM action backlog
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Theme classification + sentiment
✅ Large-text induction 
The attached file contains 200 user reviews from FinTech firm C's app over the past 30 days (with star ratings and content). Please:
1. Automatically induce 5–7 main themes
2. For each theme: count, average rating, and 2 representative quotes
3. Estimate overall NPS and sentiment score (-1 to +1)
4. Present in a Markdown table
Round 1 sample output · 07_voc_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Convert into a PM-actionable backlog
✅ Analysis → action conversion 
You are the product manager for this app. Convert the themes above into the next sprint backlog, including:
- 5 user stories (As a... I want... so that...)
- For each story: priority (P0/P1/P2), estimate (S/M/L), and the impact metric you expect to move
- 1 "absolutely cannot wait" P0 with risk justification
Present as a table.
Round 2 sample output · 07_voc_v2.png
↑ Back to top
08
⚖️

EU AI Act vs. Taiwan AI Basic Act

Data Insights

Compare excerpts of the EU AI Act with Taiwan's AI Basic Act to surface enterprise compliance gaps. 2 rounds move from comparison table to a compliance roadmap.

📎
Sample file: samples/eu_ai_act_excerpt.docx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① Cross-regulation comparison ② Gap → roadmap
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Article comparison
✅ Cross-regulation mapping 
The attached file is an excerpt of EU AI Act provisions on high-risk AI systems. Compare against the latest version of Taiwan's "Artificial Intelligence Basic Act" and produce a comparison table:
Columns: Topic / EU AI Act provision / Taiwan AI Basic Act provision / Key differences / Implications for Taiwan exporters to the EU
Cover at least 6 topics: high-risk definition, transparency, data governance, human oversight, audit logging, penalties.
Round 1 sample output · 08_aiact_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Compliance roadmap
✅ Gap → roadmap 
Based on the comparison above, suppose you are the chief compliance officer at Tier-1 foundry A (anonymized). Produce an H1 2026 AI-compliance roadmap:
- 3 must-do items (with owning function and target quarter)
- 2 should-do items
- Top 3 expected compliance risks and mitigations
- Present as a Gantt-style table (quarter × task)
Round 2 sample output · 08_aiact_v2.png
↑ Back to top
09
📊

Bid Deck Outline + Speaker Notes

Content Creation

Based on RFP highlights and the company's strengths, produce a 12-slide bid-deck outline with speaker notes. 2 rounds move from skeleton to detail.

📎
Sample file: samples/rfp_extract.docx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① Story arc ② Speaker notes with emotional pacing
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · 12-slide skeleton
✅ Story-arc thinking 
The attached file is a summary of an RFP from a Taiwan government agency for an AI platform. Produce a 12-slide bid-deck outline (each slide: title + 3 bullets + 1 punchline):

1. Cover / 2. Understanding of the problem / 3-4. Why now (market pain) / 5-6. Core solution / 7-8. Technical differentiation (incl. strategy for combining a domestic Taiwan-language model) / 9. Past case studies / 10. Team / 11. Timeline and milestones / 12. Why us (close with 3 reasons)
Round 1 sample output · 09_pitch_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Add speaker notes and emotional pacing
✅ Speaker-flow design 
For slides 5, 7, and 12, add speaker notes (150–200 words each) and label:
- The core emotion the slide should convey (e.g., trust / urgency / resonance)
- Suggested pause points
- One question to engage the evaluation panel
Goal: in 2 minutes the presenter makes the panel remember these 3 slides.
Round 2 sample output · 09_pitch_v2.png
↑ Back to top
10
🏛️

Policy Consultation Response (formal Taiwan gov't style)

Content Creation

Respond to MoDA Taiwan's draft on AI risk classification. 3 rounds move from draft → formal Taiwan government writing style → press-conference talking points.

💡
Advanced techniques: ① Formal Taiwan-government writing style ② Risk-spectrum argumentation ③ Conversion to media talking points
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Position and three core arguments
✅ Structured advocacy 
Suppose I am the chairperson of an industry association responding to MoDA Taiwan's public consultation on the "AI Risk Classification and Management Draft". My position is "support risk classification, but oppose classifying all general-purpose large language models as high-risk by default". Produce a draft response:
- Opening position (3 sentences)
- Three core arguments, 200 words each (industry competitiveness / international comparison / room for innovation)
- Three concrete legislative recommendations (bullets)
- Closing offer to collaborate
Round 1 sample output · 10_policy_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Convert to formal Taiwan government writing style
✅ Register conversion 
Rewrite the draft above in the formal Taiwan government document format (recipient / subject / explanation / proposed actions), using concise, professional wording aligned with Taiwan public-sector writing conventions.
Round 2 sample output · 10_policy_v2.png
R3Round 3 · Convert into media talking points
✅ Cross-channel rewrite 
The chairperson will sit for a 5-minute interview with a major financial media outlet tomorrow. Convert the response into 5 sound bites (each deliverable in under 30 seconds, around 60 words). They should be sharp, vivid, and headline-ready, but must not attack the government or call out any official by name.
Round 3 sample output · 10_policy_v3.png
↑ Back to top
11
👥

Same Message, 4 Audiences

Content Creation

Rewrite the same announcement "the company is rolling out AI tools next year" into 4 versions for the CEO, engineers, sales, and customers. Single round, but produce all four at once.

💡
Advanced technique: ✅ Parallel multi-version output + audience-context switching
Prompt iteration
R1Single round · 4 audiences in parallel
✅ Multi-version output 
Source message: "The company has decided to roll out Microsoft 365 Copilot company-wide in Q1 next year. Estimated investment: NT$80 million. Goal: 25% per-capita productivity uplift within 18 months. Mandatory 8 hours of training for all employees."

Produce 4 versions in one go, each under 200 words:

【Version 1 · For the CEO】Tone: strategic lens, ROI focus, controlled risk
【Version 2 · For engineers】Tone: technical detail, no impact on dev velocity, privacy and security
【Version 3 · For sales】Tone: how much more revenue can we generate, how customers will perceive this, commission impact
【Version 4 · For customers】Tone: how we use AI to improve your experience, while your data remains absolutely safe

The four versions must be clearly differentiated — not just word swaps.
Round 1 sample output · 11_audience.png
↑ Back to top
12
🗺️

AI Supply-Chain Map (Mermaid)

Content Creation

Use Mermaid to draw the 2026 global AI-server supply chain, covering GPU / HBM / advanced packaging / liquid cooling. 2 rounds move from architecture to risk overlay.

💡
Advanced techniques: ① Mermaid flowchart ② Risk visualization
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Base supply chain
✅ Mermaid diagram 
Using Mermaid `graph LR` syntax, draw a "2026 global AI-server supply-chain map" with at least 5 layers:
1. Upstream key materials (HBM, advanced-process wafers)
2. Core silicon design (GPU, ASIC, CPU)
3. Advanced packaging (CoWoS, SoIC)
4. System integration (server ODMs, liquid-cooling modules)
5. End applications (cloud hyperscalers, sovereign AI national programs)

Use generic anonymized labels for each node (e.g., "Major GPU vendor", "Korean memory maker", "Taiwan ODM", "North American cloud provider") — do not name any real companies. Output the raw Mermaid code only.
Round 1 sample output · 12_supply_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Overlay geopolitical risk
✅ Risk overlay 
On the Mermaid diagram above, please:
- Highlight the 3 nodes with the highest geopolitical risk in red (e.g., `style ... fill:#fee`)
- Highlight the 2 capacity-bottleneck nodes in yellow
- Below the diagram, list the specific risk for each red/yellow node (earthquake / export controls / single supplier, etc.)
Still output code that pastes directly into the Mermaid live editor.
Round 2 sample output · 12_supply_v2.png
↑ Back to top
13
📋

RFP Decomposition into Work Breakdown

Process Automation

Decompose a 30-page RFP summary into a WBS, RACI, and risk register. 3 rounds at PM-grade detail.

📎
Sample file: samples/rfp_extract.docx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① WBS decomposition ② RACI matrix ③ Risk quantification
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Three-level WBS
✅ Structured decomposition 
The attached file is the RFP summary for a government AI platform. Decompose it into a three-level WBS (Work Breakdown Structure), formatted as:
1.x Major phase
  1.x.x Work package
    1.x.x.x Task
At least 5 major phases, 2–4 work packages per phase, and 2–3 tasks per work package. Tag each task with an estimated person-days.
Round 1 sample output · 13_rfp_v1.png
R2Round 2 · RACI matrix
✅ Responsibility assignment matrix 
Based on the WBS above, produce a RACI matrix (R-Responsible, A-Accountable, C-Consulted, I-Informed). Roles include: PM, tech lead, data engineer, compliance, UX, customer-side stakeholder. Present as a table of work packages × roles.
Round 2 sample output · 13_rfp_v2.png
R3Round 3 · Quantitative risk register
✅ Quantified risk log 
List the top 10 risks for this project, each with the following columns (table format): risk description / probability (1-5) / impact (1-5) / risk score (probability × impact) / response strategy (Avoid / Mitigate / Transfer / Accept) / early warning signal. Highlight the top 3 red-flag risks that must be resolved before kick-off.
Round 3 sample output · 13_rfp_v3.png
↑ Back to top
14
✅

Meeting Transcript → Action List

Process Automation

Convert a 90-minute AI-strategy meeting transcript into decisions, action items, and follow-up emails. 2 rounds move from extraction to send-ready output.

📎
Sample file: samples/meeting_transcript.docx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① Long-text decision extraction ② Auto-generate downstream communications
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Decisions and action list
✅ Long-text extraction 
The attached file is the transcript of a 90-minute AI Strategy Committee meeting. Please extract:

【Decisions】(up to 5, each one sentence)
【Action Items】Format: owner / task / due date / success metric
【Parking Lot (unresolved)】
【Suggested Agenda for Next Meeting】

Present in Markdown. Provide at least 8 action items.
Round 1 sample output · 14_action_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Auto-generate follow-up emails
✅ Cross-artifact generation 
Based on the action list above, produce two follow-up emails (in English, formal but not stiff):

【Email 1 · To all attendees】Summarize decisions and each person's action items, attach the action-item table, ask everyone to confirm within 48 hours
【Email 2 · To the CEO who did not attend】3-paragraph summary: strategic conclusions, the two issues that need a CEO call, what 30 minutes of CEO time is needed for next week

Both subject lines must be concise and vivid.
Round 2 sample output · 14_action_v2.png
↑ Back to top
15
🔐

ISO 27001 Internal Audit Checklist

Process Automation

Produce an audit checklist and evidence requirements for ISO 27001:2022 Annex A controls. 2 rounds move from checklist to interview script.

💡
Advanced techniques: ① Standards mapping ② Audit interview simulation
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Control audit checklist
✅ Standards mapping 
For each of the 4 themes of ISO 27001:2022 Annex A (A.5 Organizational controls / A.6 People controls / A.7 Physical controls / A.8 Technological controls), pick the 5 most critical controls and produce an audit checklist table:
Columns: control ID / control name / audit point (1 sentence) / expected evidence (3 items) / common deficiencies
Approximately 20 rows in total.
Round 1 sample output · 15_iso_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Audit interview script
✅ Conversation simulation 
Pick the 5 highest-risk controls from the 20 above. For each, design a 3-turn auditor vs. auditee dialogue:
- Q1 (warm-up, open-ended)
- Q2 (evidence-focused, specific)
- Q3 (test how it actually works in practice, follow-up)
Label a "common wrong answer" and a "green-light example answer" for each. To be used for training new auditors.
Round 2 sample output · 15_iso_v2.png
↑ Back to top
16
📚

Travel Policy KB Q&A

Process Automation

Upload the company travel policy and simulate 5 employee scenarios — require precise answers + clause citations + judgment on grey areas. 2 rounds move from direct answers to handling exceptions.

📎
Sample file: samples/travel_policy.docx
💡
Advanced techniques: ① KB grounding ② Citations + grey-area judgment
Prompt iteration
R1Round 1 · Five-scenario Q&A
✅ Document-grounded Q&A 
The attached file is the company "2026 Travel and Entertainment Policy". Act as the company travel-policy AI assistant and answer the following 5 common employee questions. Each answer:
- 1 paragraph direct answer (under 80 words)
- Cite the clause number
- If there is a proviso, call it out clearly

Questions:
1. Can I fly business class on a trip to India?
2. What is the per-head cap for client entertainment dinners? Can alcohol be expensed?
3. If I extend a weekend for personal travel, how is the airfare difference handled?
4. Can a cross-team gathering be expensed?
5. How should an Uber/taxi above NTD 1,500 be handled?
Round 1 sample output · 16_kb_v1.png
R2Round 2 · Grey-area judgment
✅ Exception handling + escalation 
The next 3 are grey areas not explicitly covered by the policy. As the AI assistant, give "the most reasonable recommendation + why + whether pre-approval is required + suggested escalation contact":
1. On a Silicon Valley trip, the client booked the hotel at USD 480/night (policy cap is USD 350). What now?
2. NTD 8,000 round of golf with a client — entertainment or business development?
3. A USD 20 webinar ticket to learn a new technology online — which expense category?

Answers must be conservative, auditable, and protect the employee.
Round 2 sample output · 16_kb_v2.png
↑ Back to top
